title: Context managers

canonicalExplanation.summary: A context manager's enter and exit protocol brackets resource use so cleanup runs on normal completion and exception unwinding; with binds the enter result, not necessarily the manager itself.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A file is opened with a with statement and is closed even when parsing raises halfway through the suite. Your goal is to refactor manual file cleanup into a failure-safe context-managed workflow. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Function contracts and returns; Unicode text and formatting; Files, paths and JSON; Exception handling and propagation. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A context manager's enter and exit protocol brackets resource use so cleanup runs on normal completion and exception unwinding; with binds the enter result, not necessarily the manager itself. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: __exit__ can suppress an exception by returning true, cleanup can fail, ownership determines who closes, multiple contexts unwind in reverse order, and async resources use a separate protocol. The learner must reject this documented misconception: with is only shorthand for close at the final line and every __exit__ return value re-raises exceptions.

includes: Use with-statements to guarantee resource cleanup and understand the enter/exit protocol conceptually.
Refactor manual file cleanup into a failure-safe context-managed workflow.
excludes: __exit__ can suppress an exception by returning true, cleanup can fail, ownership determines who closes, multiple contexts unwind in reverse order, and async resources use a separate protocol.

outcomes: Refactor manual file cleanup into a failure-safe context-managed workflow.

examples.title: Context managers: direct language case
examples.situation: A file is opened with a with statement and is closed even when parsing raises halfway through the suite.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A file is opened with a with statement and is closed even when parsing raises halfway through the suite.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Use with to guarantee protocol-driven cleanup during all exits, and return false from __exit__ unless the manager deliberately and safely suppresses the exception.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Use with to guarantee protocol-driven cleanup during all exits, and return false from __exit__ unless the manager deliberately and safely suppresses the exception.

examples.title: Context managers: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A custom transaction context rolls back on an exception, commits on success, and returns false so unexpected exceptions keep propagating.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A custom transaction context rolls back on an exception, commits on success, and returns false so unexpected exceptions keep propagating.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: __exit__ can suppress an exception by returning true, cleanup can fail, ownership determines who closes, multiple contexts unwind in reverse order, and async resources use a separate protocol.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: with is only shorthand for close at the final line and every __exit__ return value re-raises exceptions.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Refactor manual file cleanup into a failure-safe context-managed workflow.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: with is only shorthand for close at the final line and every __exit__ return value re-raises exceptions.
misconceptions.correction: Use with to guarantee protocol-driven cleanup during all exits, and return false from __exit__ unless the manager deliberately and safely suppresses the exception.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: with is only shorthand for close at the final line and every __exit__ return value re-raises exceptions.

analogy.example: a records office that escalates named failures, validates decoded forms, and returns borrowed resources on every exit can introduce Context managers, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: exceptions, custom failure categories, files, JSON, and contexts
limitations: exception chaining, suppression, path resolution, encoding, partial writes, and protocol methods are software rules
